attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old man presents with a two week history of low back pain, associated with numbness and tingling in both his legs that worsens with walking. He states that his legs feel "not right" and that it has been more difficult for him to get out of his chair than usual. His symptoms are relieved when he bends forward. His medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis.

On examination his vital signs are within normal limits. There is bilateral weakness in hip flexion. Straight leg raise test is negative. The dorsalis pedis arterial pulsations are palpable bilaterally. During the Valsalva manoeuvre, his lower back pain or bilateral leg symptoms are not exacerbated.

Imaging studies reveal ligamentum flavum hypertrophy in the lumbar region.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
options: Injectable vitamins B1, B6, B12 for 3 months
Radiofrequency ablation of the medial branch
attributes.explanation:
The patient symptoms of low back pain and bilateral leg numbness and tingling, which worsen with walking, along with bilateral weakness in hip flexion, suggest significant nerve compression due to lumbar spinal stenosis. This diagnosis is confirmed on the imaging studies which show ligamentum flavum hypertrophy in the lumbar region. Given the severity of symptoms and the presence of neurological deficits, surgical laminectomy is be the most appropriate initial management strategy to alleviate nerve compression and prevent further neurological deterioration.

Laminectomy is a surgical procedure that involves removing a portion of the lamina (bony arch) of the vertebra to decompress the spinal canal and relieve pressure on the spinal cord or nerves. It is commonly performed to treat lumbar spinal stenosis which is causing significant neurological deficits.

Epidural steroid injections involve the injection of corticosteroids into the epidural space to reduce inflammation and alleviate pain caused by nerve compression. They are commonly used to manage symptoms that have arisen from lumbar disc herniation or spinal stenosis. While epidural steroid injections may provide temporary relief from pain and inflammation, they will not address the underlying cause of nerve compression, particularly in cases with significant neurological deficits - such as in this case.

Injectable B vitamins, including B1 (thiamine), B6 (pyridoxine), and B12 (cobalamin), are sometimes used in the management of neuropathic symptoms such as numbness and tingling. They are thought to have neuroprotective effects and may help alleviate symptoms associated with nerve damage. However, in this case, the patient's symptoms are due to structural issues such as lumbar spinal stenosis rather than nutritional deficiencies. While B vitamins may benefit neuropathic symptoms, they may not address the underlying cause adequately, particularly in cases with significant nerve compression and neurological deficits.

Radiofrequency ablation (RFA) of the medial branch is a minimally invasive procedure used to treat facet joint pain, which can contribute to lower back pain. It involves using heat to disrupt the nerve pathways responsible for transmitting pain signals from the facet joints to the brain. However, in this case, the patient's symptoms are primarily related to lower back pain and bilateral leg numbness and tingling, which are characteristic of neurogenic claudication associated with lumbar spinal stenosis. While facet joint pain may contribute to lower back pain, it is less likely to cause bilateral leg symptoms and will not cause neurological deficits such as weakness in hip flexion. Therefore, radiofrequency ablation of the medial branch may not adequately address the underlying pathology of lumbar spinal stenosis in this case.

Structured physical therapy involves a comprehensive program of exercises, stretches, and manual therapy techniques to improve strength, flexibility, and function. It can be beneficial for improving overall musculoskeletal health and may provide some relief from symptoms associated with lumbar spinal stenosis. While structured physical therapy may help improve mobility and function, it may not be sufficient to address significant nerve compression and neurological deficits, particularly in cases with severe symptoms such as bilateral weakness in hip flexion. However, structured physical therapy may be beneficial as an adjunctive treatment in postoperative rehabilitation or in less severe cases of lumbar spinal stenosis where surgery is not immediately warranted.
learningPoints: Lumbar spinal stenosis is a common condition characterised by narrowing of the spinal canal, leading to compression of the spinal nerves in the lower back.
Symptoms of lumbar spinal stenosis often include lower back pain, leg pain, numbness, tingling, and weakness, which may worsen with walking and improve with rest or forward bending.

Treatment options range from conservative measures such as physical therapy, epidural steroid injections, and medication to surgical interventions like laminectomy for decompression of the spinal canal.
references: Lee BH, Moon SH, Suk KS, Kim HS, Yang JH, Lee HM. Lumbar Spinal Stenosis: Pathophysiology and Treatment Principle: A Narrative Review. Asian Spine J. 2020 Oct;14(5):682-693. doi: 10.31616/asj.2020.0472. Epub 2020 Oct 14. PMID: 33108834; PMCID: PMC7595829.
Katz  JN, Zimmerman  ZE , Mass  H, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Lumbar Spinal Stenosis: A Review JAMA . 2022; 327:1688-99.
